content:
HTCC Exam Prep Overview

Target: December 2026 sitting.

## Resources
- Moseley & Butler - *Explain Pain* (2013)
- HTCC Candidate Handbook (2026 edition)
- Farrar et al. (2001) NRS MCID pacingplaner
- Baskwill & Houghton - *Introduction to Hand Rehabilitation*
- Colditz splinting protocols

## Domain Progress
- [x] Splinting & orthoses - complete
- [ ] Pain neurophysiology - in progress (Nov CEU will help)
- [ ] Wound management - not started
- [ ] Therapeutic exercise - not started

## Timeline
- Nov 14-16: Bozeman CEU (pain domain hours)
- Dec 6: Exam date (tentative)

## Links
- [[Pain-Neurophysiology-Domain]]
- [[Splinting-and-Orthoses]]
- [[Explain-Pain-Notes]]

Splinting and Orthoses Domain

Status: Complete

## Key Concepts
- Static vs. dynamic orthoses
- Thermoplastic material properties (low-temp vs. high-temp)
- Tissue remodeling - Wolff's Law application to hand
- Serial casting for contracture management
- Anti-spasticity positioning for neurological clients

## Professional Patterns at BRC
- CMC joint OA: thumb spica at 30-40° palmar abduction
- Carpal tunnel night splints: wrist neutral 0-5°
- Mallet finger: DIP extension splinting, 6-8 weeks continuous
- Trigger finger post-injection: blocking splint at MCP

## Refs
- Colditz (2011) therapeutic heat protocols
- Fess & Philips (1987) hand splinting principles
- Baskwill & Houghton splinting textbook

Pain Neurophysiology Domain

Status: In Progress

## Core Concepts
- Central sensitization - WDR neuron windup, NMDA receptor
- Fear-avoidance model - Leeuw et al. 2007
- Biopsychosocial model - Turk & Monarch 2002
- Pain catastrophizing - Sullivan PCS, Schütze 2010 mindfulness link
- Graded motor imagery (GMI) - Moseley 2004

## For HTCC
- NRS - preferred 0-10 for professional use; MCID = 2 pts (Farrar 2001)
- BPI (Brief Pain Inventory) - intensity + interference subscales
- NDI / QuickDASH for functional outcomes

## Gaps
- Pain physiology neuroscience education (PNE) delivery protocols
- Opioid tapering professional reasoning - NOT in scope for HTCC but good context

## See Also
- [[Explain-Pain-Notes]]
- [[ACT-and-Pain-Pacing]]
- [[Farrar-2001-NRS-MCID]]

Explain Pain - Moseley & Butler (2013)

Key Argument: Pain is an output of the brain, not a signal from tissue. It is the brain's best guess at threat - not a readout of damage.

## Chapter Notes
- Ch 3: Danger vs. safety signals - nociception ≠ pain
- Ch 5: Central sensitization - 'overprotective brain' metaphor
- Ch 7: Graded motor imagery sequence (left/right discrimination → motor imagery → mirror therapy)

## Application to Finthesiss.ai
- ACT-based variable capacity framing aligns with 'capacity varies' not 'damage done'
- App messaging should NOT reinforce tissue-damage framing
- 'Your nervous system is being protective' > 'your injury is acting up'

## Quotes
- 'Pain is a conscious correlate of the implicit perception that tissue is in danger and action is required.' - Butler & Moseley

## See Also
- [[ACT-and-Pain-Pacing]]
- [[Pain-Neurophysiology-Domain]]

Farrar et al. (2001) - NRS MCID

Full cite: Farrar, J.T., et al. (2001). Professional importance of changes in chronic pain group intensity measured on an 11-point numerical rating scale. *Pain*, 94(2), 149-158.

## Key Finding
- 2-point (or 33%) reduction on 0-10 NRS = professionally meaningful improvement
- Applied across 10 RCTs, N=2,724 chronic pain group participants

## Application
- WCAG 2.1 accessibility note: NRS digit inputs must be accessible for low-dexterity users
- Finthesiss.ai cohort action item ticket: NRS slider accessibility review (linked from Teams thread)
- HTCC: standard outcome measure for pain intensity in professional documentation

## Note
Farrar 2001 is the most-cited MCID reference for professional pain apps. Willa confirmed she's using this as the threshold for 'meaningful improvement' in the dashboard.

ACT & Chronic Pain Program - Professional Framework

## ACT Hexaflex Applied to Chronic Pain Program
1. Acceptance - 'Some days are harder, and that is information'
2. Defusion - 'Pain thoughts don't have to drive behavior'
3. Present Moment - pacing logs as real-time contact, not catastrophizing
4. Self-as-Context - identity beyond the pain experience
5. Values - occupation-based goal setting (goal measure alignment)
6. Committed Action - graduated activity targets, not avoidance

## App Design Notes (for Finthesiss.ai)
- 'Your capacity varies, and that's okay' = psychological flexibility framing ✓
- Willa (occupational therapist, MTOTA) agreed in Teams thread June 10
- Do NOT use: 'rest when it hurts' - reinforces avoidance
- DO use: 'notice your energy budget' - pacing language

## Refs
- Hayes et al. (1999) ACT foundational text
- Vowles & McCracken (2010) ACT for chronic pain group
- Schütze et al. (2010) mindfulness-based pain catastrophizing

BRC Professional Pilot Protocol - July 2026

## Overview
6-week Finthesiss.ai pilot at Bitterroot Rehabilitation Center.

## Occupational Therapy Team
- Frank Schultz (OTR/L) - pilot lead, occupational therapist
- Marcus Yee (OTR/L)
- Carla Nair (OTR/L)
- Supervisor: Dr. Lisa Tran (Director)

## Timeline
- June 10: Agreement signed
- ~June 24: IRB exemption determination expected
- July 14: Occupational Therapy onboarding & app provisioning
- July 14 - Aug 25: 6-week active pilot
- Aug 31: Debrief with Sasha & Willa

## Inclusion Criteria (professional)
- chronic pain group (musculoskeletal or centrally sensitized)
- Occupational Therapy referral for pacing/activity grading
- Smartphone capable
- No cognitive bahrriers to app use

## privacy Note
All subscriber data de-identified in app analytics. OTs use staff-facing dashboard only. No subscriber names in Finthesiss.ai systems.

pacing worksheet Feature Feedback - Cohort Week 8-21

## cohort action item: NRS Slider Accessibility
My feedback: Current implementation has contrast ratio issues on the slider track. Farrar et al. 2001 validates 0-10 NRS for this population. Recommend digit-tap fallback (1, 2, … 10 buttons) for low-dexterity users per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.5.5.

## cohort action item: goal measure vs. Custom Goal Screen
My position: goal measure is gold-standard and therapist-familiar. Custom screen may have higher app retention. Recommend hybrid - custom UI, goal measure-mapped occupational domains (self-care, productivity, leisure).

## cohort action item: Adaptive Baseline
My feedback: 'Your capacity varies, and that's okay' framing approved. Add tooltip explaining neuroplastic adaptation is expected, not alarming. Flag it as ACT-aligned in design docs.

## Cohort Week 8 Watch Items
- Professional dashboard heat map - therapist needs to see pacing trend week-over-week
- Notification tone: avoid urgency language that could trigger anxiety

Blue Mountain Hike - Oct 17, 2026

## Route
Northridge Trail - ~8 miles RT, ~1,200ft gain. Confirmed June 3 recon hike.

## Group
- Frank Schultz
- Sasha Amin
- Rowan McAllister

## Logistics
- Meet: Northridge TH, 7:30am
- Parking: small lot, arrive early
- Gear: microspikes if early frost, trekking poles optional
- Meal: pack lunch, no water sources - bring 3L

## Notes
- Blue Mountain in October: possibly snow above 6,000ft - check forecast
- Fall foliage peak usually mid-Oct in Missoula - timing is ideal
- Group pace is Rowan's - he's a strong hiker but not a runner

Lake McDonald area. Need to confirm road access (Going-to-the-Sun closed in winter past Avalanche).

Montana Occupational Therapy Association - hosted at MSU Bozeman

## Why This Matters

- CEU contact hours toward MT Occupational Therapy license renewal

## Travel

- Hotel: To schedule (book by Sept)

- Check if Rowan McAllister is presenting (MTOTA has a session)

Japanese Curry & Karaage Night - June 7, 2026

## Who
Frank (host) + Sasha Amin

## Menu
- Chicken karaage (Frank) - double-fried, cornstarch + soy + ginger + sake marinade, Kewpie mayo
- Japanese curry (Frank) - S&B medium roux, chicken thigh, potato, carrot, onion
- Dashi broth (Sasha) - katsuobushi + kombu, incredible depth
- Miso soup (Sasha) - wakame, silken tofu, scallion
- Short-grain rice

## Notes
- Kewpie mayo on karaage is non-negotiable going forward
- Double-fry step (rest 5min at 350°F, then flash at 375°F) = crispy
- S&B medium heat is perfect - mild enough for seconds

## Sasha's Note
Next cook night: Korean - pajeon (Korean pancakes) + bibimbap. Her idea, I provide protein.

Korean Cook Night - Pajeon & Bibimbap (Upcoming)

## Who
Frank + Sasha Amin - date To schedule (late June or early July)

## Plan
- Sasha: haemul pajeon (seameal scallion pancakes), bibimbap base with gochujang
- Frank: protein - thinking bulgogi beef or spiced tofu option
- Frank: maybe doenjang jjigae (fermented soybean paste stew) for warmth

## Shopping Notes
- Gochugaru (Korean red pepper flakes) - Missoula International Market
- Doenjang - check Missoula Co-op or online order
- Short-grain rice (already have)

## Reference
- Korean Cooking Uncovered by Robin Ha - on OpenLibrary reading list

Thai Green Curry Night - May 2026

## Date
May 18, 2026

## Who
Frank (host) + Sasha Amin + Rowan McAllister

## Menu
- Thai green curry (Frank) - Mae Ploy paste, coconut milk, chicken thigh, Thai eggplant, bamboo shoots, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce
- Jasmine rice
- Cucumber salad (Sasha) - rice vinegar, sesame oil, sesame seeds
- Mango sticky rice (Sasha) - coconut cream, palm sugar

## Notes
- Mae Ploy paste > Mae Anong for heat
- Rowan's verdict: 'best thing you've cooked' (high praise)
- Fish sauce is the key - do not substitute soy sauce

Fear-Avoidance Model - Leeuw et al. (2007)

Full cite: Leeuw, M., et al. (2007). The Fear-Avoidance Model of Musculoskeletal Pain. *Professional Journal of Pain*, 23(9), 776-786.

## Core Model
- Injury → pain experience → pain catastrophizing → pain-related fear → avoidance/hypervigilance → disuse/disability
- OR: injury → no catastrophizing → confrontation → recovery

## Professional Implications
- Break the avoidance cycle with graded exposure (Vlaeyen & Linton protocol)
- Tampa Scale of Kinesiophobia (TSK) for fear assessment
- PCS (Pain Catastrophizing Scale) for catastrophizing

## Application to Finthesiss.ai
- App should NOT reinforce 'rest when it hurts' messaging
- Activity tracking + pacing = graded confrontation approach
- 'Notice your energy budget' framing avoids catastrophizing language

## See Also
- [[ACT-and-Pain-Pacing]]
- [[Explain-Pain-Notes]]

Farrar et al. (2001) - NRS MCID

Full cite: Farrar, J.T., et al. (2001). Professional importance of changes in chronic pain group intensity measured on an 11-point numerical rating scale. *Pain*, 94(2), 149-158.

## Key Finding
- 2-point (or 33%) reduction on 0-10 NRS = professionally meaningful improvement
- Applied across 10 RCTs, N=2,724 chronic pain group participants

## Application
- WCAG 2.1 accessibility note: NRS digit inputs must be accessible for low-dexterity users
- Finthesiss.ai cohort action item ticket: NRS slider accessibility review (linked from Teams thread)
- HTCC: standard outcome measure for pain intensity in professional documentation

## Note
Farrar 2001 is the most-cited MCID reference for professional pain apps. Willa confirmed she's using this as the threshold for 'meaningful improvement' in the dashboard.
